data.title: LQWD Technologies Acquires Additional Bitcoin Increasing its Holdings to ~262 BTC
data.description:
Vancouver, British Columbia--(Newsfile Corp. - April 15, 2026) - LQWD Technologies Corp. (OTCQX: LQWDF) ("LQWD" or the "Company"), a Canadian-based Bitcoin-backed company and provider of enterprise-grade infrastructure for the Bitcoin Lightning Network, is pleased to announce the acquisition of an additional 9.5 Bitcoin to its treasury.

Details are as follows:

  • Numbers of Bitcoin Purchased: 9.5 Bitcoin
  • Average Purchase Price: ~US$77,653 per Bitcoin
  • Amount Purchased: US ~$737,708
  • Total Bitcoin Holdings: ~262 Bitcoin
  • Total Average Purchase Price: ~US$77,379 per Bitcoin
  • Total Amount Purchased: ~US$20,273,508

The Company now holds approximately 262 Bitcoin which equates to approximately 822 Sats per LQWD share outstanding, or 614 Sats per share on a fully diluted basis.

"At LQWD, we took advantage of lower Bitcoin prices and staggered our purchases over the past months to continue increasing our Bitcoin holdings, which we actively deploy to support and scale our growing Lightning Network infrastructure," said Shone Anstey, CEO of LQWD.

LQWD has 31,863,408 shares outstanding, carries no debt, and maintains its Bitcoin reserves entirely unencumbered.

About LQWD Technologies Corp.

LQWD offers investors unique exposure to both the long-term appreciation of Bitcoin and the emerging machine-to-machine economy powered by Lightning-based transactions. The Company is positioning Bitcoin not just as an asset, but as a global, monetizable network, accelerating its evolution from a store of value into a scalable payment system through the Lightning Network, a second-layer protocol enabling instant, low-cost transactions at internet scale.

With a strategic Bitcoin treasury deployed as productive network capital and AI-driven infrastructure optimizing routing, liquidity, and performance, LQWD is positioned at the forefront of scalable, real-time, fee-generating Bitcoin payments.

LQWD is a Canadian-domiciled public company with offices in Vancouver, Canada, and Lugano, Switzerland. The Company has approximately 31.9 million shares outstanding and 42.7 million shares fully diluted and maintains a strong balance sheet, holding ~262 Bitcoin with no outstanding debt, convertible bonds, or debentures.

The Company's shares trade in Canada on the TSX Venture Exchange under the symbol LQWD, and on the OTCQX Market in the United States under the symbol LQWDF.

For more information, please visit LQWD's corporate presentation and connect with the Company's Lightning Network nodes in real time.

For further information:

Ashley Garnot, President/Director

Phone: 1.604.669.0912

Email: ashley@lqwdtech.com

Website: www.lqwdtech.com

X: @LQWDTech

Disclaimer

Forward-Looking Statements

This release contains "forward-looking information" within the meaning of applicable securities laws. Forward-looking information in this release includes, but is not limited to, statements regarding the Company's Bitcoin treasury strategy, the deployment of Bitcoin reserves to support and scale its Lightning Network infrastructure, the growth of Lightning-based transactions, the monetization of the Bitcoin network, the Company's ability to optimize routing, liquidity and network performance using AI-driven infrastructure, and the Company's positioning within the emerging machine-to-machine economy. Although the Company believes, considering the experience of its officers and directors, current conditions and expected future developments and other factors that have been considered appropriate, that the expectations reflected in this forward-looking information are reasonable, undue reliance should not be placed on them because the Company can give no assurance that they will prove to be correct. Actual results and developments may differ materially from those contemplated by these statements. The statements in this press release are made as of the date of this release and the Company assumes no responsibility to update them or revise them to reflect new events or circumstances other than as required by applicable securities laws.

Neither the TSX Venture Exchange nor its Regulation Services Provider (as that term is defined in the policies of the TSX Venture Exchange) accepts responsibility for the adequacy or accuracy of this release.

data.title: Hump day data roundup: Builder sentiment, mortgage demand, import prices, Empire State
data.description:
Key points:
  • Nasdaq up ~0.8%, S&P 500 green; Dow modestly lower
  • Tech leads S&P 500 sector gainers'; Materials weakest group
  • Euro STOXX 600 index off ~0.3%
  • Dollar ~flat; bitcoin slips; gold dips; crude rises
  • US 10-year Treasury yield edges up to ~4.27%

HUMP DAY DATA ROUNDUP: HOMEBUILDER SENTIMENT, MORTGAGE DEMAND, IMPORT PRICES, EMPIRE STATE

Investors celebrated income tax day with a handful of mixed data from different corners of the economy.

Starting with the housing market, the mood among homebuilders tanked this month.

The National Association of Home Builders' (USNAHB=ECI) housing market index (HMI) dropped four points to print at 34, a lot more dour than the one-point drop economists predicted.

With this, builder sentiment has been sour for two full years, having languished south of 50 - the dividing line between pessimism and optimism - since April 2024.

“Builder sentiment has fallen back in spring as buyers face ongoing elevated interest rates and growing economic uncertainty,” writes NAHB Chairman Bill Owens. “The year started with hopes for housing momentum growth, but risks with respect to the Iran war, energy costs, and declines for consumer confidence have slowed the market."

Indeed, with building permits in steady decline, home sales weakening and 30-year fixed mortgage rates coasting above 6%, there's little to coax would-be homebuyers into the fray.

Speaking of the devil, the cost of financing home loans eased enough last week to tempt a few would-be refi borrowers from the sidelines, according to the Mortgage Bankers Association (MBA).

The average 30-year fixed contract rate (USMG=ECI) - which tends to rise and fall in tandem with benchmark Treasury yields - shed 9 basis points to 6.42%.

Even so, demand for loans to purchase homes (USMGPI=ECI) - among the housing market's most forward-looking indicators - dipped by 1.0%. But refi applications (USMGR=ECI), which accounted for 45.5% of all applications, saved the day by rising 5.1%.

Taken together, total mortgage demand inched 1.8% higher.

"This dip in rates helped to support an increase in conventional refinance applications, which had declined for five consecutive weeks," says Joel Kan, MBA's deputy chief economist. "Purchase activity remained subdued as potential homebuyers remained hesitant given the current economic uncertainty."

The 30-year fixed rate currently sits 39 basis points below where it was during the same week a year ago.

Over that same period, purchase applications have dipped by 2.9%, while refi demand has risen by 14.8%.

The cost of goods imported to the United States (USIMP=ECI) (excluding tariffs) rose by 0.8% in March, per Labor Department data, well shy of the 2.0% consensus.

This follows February's downward revision, to 0.9% from 1.3%.

Diving below the surface, the 9.4% jump in imported gasoline prices is unsurprising due to war-related disruption. Excluding petroleum, import prices rose by a mere 0.1%.

Year-over-year, import prices have grown by 2.1%, an abrupt acceleration from the previous month's 1.3% annual growth rate.

Import/export prices differ from other major inflation indicators with things like currency exchange rates, foreign demand and geopolitical developments thrown into the mix.

Here's a chart that shows annual import/export price growth against the dollar index, which tracks the greenback against a basket of world currencies.

Moving to manufacturing, factory activity in New York State has unexpectedly expanded this month.

The New York Fed's Empire State index (USEMPM=ECI) landed at an even 11.0 in defiance of the -0.5 reading analysts expected.

A positive Empire State reading indicates monthly expansion; a negative print signifies contraction.

A look at the details shows the general business conditions element touched its highest level since 2023, and the employment metrics show a "moderate increase in employment levels and a significant increase in hours worked," according to the press release.

On the other hand, the component measuring near-term expectations dipped and prices paid ticked higher.

"Manufacturing activity grew moderately in New York State in April," says Richard Deitz, economic research advisor at the New York Fed. "However, input price increases accelerated, supply availability is expected to worsen, and firms became less optimistic about the outlook."

On Thursday, the Philadelphia Fed will release its Philly Fed manufacturing report, which should help flesh out the picture of the current month's Atlantic region factory activity.

data.title: The Middle East Was a Bright Spot for Luxury Brands. Not Any More. — WSJ
data.description:
By Andrea Figueras

2026 was meant to be the year that things got better for the luxury industry. Not so far.

Luxury behemoth LVMH, Gucci owner Kering and Birkin bag maker Hermès International have all warned this week about the impact of the Iran war on big spending consumers in the Middle East.

Hermès shares dropped by as much as 14% on Wednesday after the Parisian fashion house reported weaker-than-expected first-quarter sales growth. The fall put the stock on course for its sharpest decline since its 1993 initial public offering, according to FactSet data.

The downbeat update from Hermès sparked a selloff among Europe's purveyors of pricey jewelry, apparel and handbags, compounding negative headlines about the state of the sector this week from Kering and LVMH.

The luxury industry has been struggling for years with faltering demand for high-end goods against the backdrop of rising prices, trade disputes and geopolitical instability. The sector had hoped for a recovery this year, driven by better results in crucial markets such as the U.S. and China. Now the Iran war is casting a shadow over those revival hopes.

Luxury groups have invested heavily in the Middle East in recent years, building extensive retail networks in cities and transport hubs such as Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi. Brands have also increased their focus on local clients through Ramadan collections, pop-ups and events.

The Middle East now accounts for about 6% of global luxury sales — roughly in line with Japan — and was the fastest-growing region for the industry last year, expanding by about 6% while the broader sector was flat.

But the war has shut some stores, reduced international visitors and disrupted wealthy local consumers from going shopping in Europe.

Hermès on Wednesday said geopolitical tensions and fewer Middle Eastern tourists visiting France had hurt its performance in the first three months of the year. Excluding currency movements, revenue rose 5.6% in the period, down from a 9.8% increase in the previous quarter.

Wholesale activity was significantly affected by lower sales to concession stores, particularly in the Middle East and in airports, the company said. It sees a slight improvement in April as stores reopen.

The Iran war isn't the luxury industry's only problem. Sales for Hermès in Asia were weak too, rising 2% in the quarter. Luxury analysts said demand for Hermès is muted in mainland China, based on conversations they had with mall owners on recent field trips.

The French company has long been known as one of the brands most resilient to downturns, thanks in part to demand for its most coveted handbags, which outpaces the rate at which it produces them. But the downbeat quarter led some analysts to question whether its business model — where customers are encouraged to buy products to gain access to the most sought after Birkin and Kelly bags — is approaching its limits.

Hermès isn't alone in warning about the fallout from the Iran war.

Rival LVMH, the owner of Louis Vuitton and Dior, reported weaker-than-expected first-quarter earnings on Monday. It said the conflict in the Middle East had a negative impact of around 1% on organic growth for the quarter.

"What we see today is still that demand is very much down," LVMH Chief Financial Officer Cécile Cabanis said of the region. Still, for all the uncertainty related to the war, Cabanis said the company was hopeful demand would return, "What we know is that the wealth has not evaporated, " she said.

Gucci's parent Kering said Tuesday that retail revenue in the Middle East, which represents around 5% of its total, declined 11% in the first three months of the year.

"In the Middle East, it's tourist flows that are suffering more than the locals," said Kering CFO Armelle Poulou.

One positive note for luxury brands: So far, the war doesn't appear to have deterred American consumers from splashing out.

Hermès reported a 17% rise in quarterly sales in the Americas, while Kering said North America stood out as the group's strongest region.

Write to Andrea Figueras at andrea.figueras@wsj.com

data.title: New York Manufacturing Index Unexpectedly Indicates Growth In April
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - A report released by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York on Wednesday unexpectedly showed a moderate increase in New York manufacturing activity in the month of April.

The New York Fed said its general business conditions index surged to a positive 11.0 in April from a negative 0.2 in March, with a positive reading indicating growth. Economists had expected the index to dip to a negative 2.0.

The unexpected increase by the headline index partly reflected a significant turnaround by shipments, as the shipments index spiked to a positive 20.2 in April from a negative 6.9 in March.

The new orders index also shot up to 19.3 in April from 6.4 in March, with both indexes reaching their highest levels since 2023.

The New York Fed said the number of employees index rose to 9.8 in April from 5.8 in March and the average workweek index jumped to 13.7 in April from 1.9 in March, suggesting a moderate increase in employment levels and a significant increase in hours worked.

The report also said the prices paid index surged to 51.0 in April from 36.6 in March, while the prices received index inched up to 21.8 in April from 21.4 in March.

Looking ahead, the New York Fed said firms still expect conditions to improve in the months ahead, although the index for future business conditions slumped to 19.6 in April from 31.0 in March, suggesting firms were less optimistic about the outlook.

The Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia is scheduled to release its report on regional manufacturing activity on Thursday. The Philly Fed Index is expected to drop to 12.0 in April from 18.1 in March.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: US crude stocks, gasoline and distillate inventories fall, EIA says
data.description:
U.S. crude stocks, gasoline and distillate inventories fell last week, the Energy Information Administration said on Wednesday.

Crude inventories fell by 913,000 barrels to 463.8 million barrels in the week ended April 10, the EIA said, compared with analysts' expectations in a Reuters poll for a 154,000-barrel rise.

Crude stocks at the Cushing, Oklahoma, delivery hub (USOICC=ECI) fell by 1.7 million barrels in the week, the EIA said.

Oil futures pared earlier losses following the report. Global Brent crude futures were trading at $95.01 a barrel, up 22 cents, at 10:38 a.m. ET (1438 GMT), while U.S. West Texas Intermediate futures were up 34 cents, at $91.62 a barrel.

Refinery crude runs (USOICR=ECI) fell by 208,000 barrels per day in the week, the EIA said, while refinery utilization rates (USOIRU=ECI) fell by 2.4 percentage points in the week to 89.6%.

U.S. gasoline stocks (USOILG=ECI) fell by 6.3 million barrels in the week to 232.9 million barrels, the EIA said, compared with analysts' expectations in a Reuters poll for a 2.1 million-barrel draw.​

Distillate stockpiles (USOILD=ECI), which include diesel and heating oil, fell by 3.1 million barrels in the week to 111.6 million barrels, versus expectations for a 2.4 million-barrel drop, the EIA data showed.

Net U.S. crude imports (USOICI=ECI) fell by 2.11 million barrels per day, the EIA said.

data.title: Allbirds' AI pivot sends its stock soaring 300%. We've seen this film before.
data.description:
By Britney Nguyen

It's not unprecedented for struggling companies to latch onto the hot trend of the moment. Remember the blockchain hype cycle?

Allbirds plans to acquire GPUs and offer compute services.

After losing its relevance as a purveyor of tech-bro sneakers, Allbirds wants to become an artificial-intelligence company - and its stock is up more than 300% on record volume.

There's precedent for companies to attempt dramatic - or desperate -pivots in an attempt to capitalize on hot trends. This was a popular strategy in late 2017 and early 2018, as businesses sought to hop on the blockchain bandwagon.

For Long Blockchain, formerly Long Island Iced Tea, the move didn't exactly pan out. Its stock initially soared on the pivot but was delisted less than a year later. Riot Platforms, once a diagnostic-equipment company that turned to bitcoin mining and then to artificial-intelligence compute, has fared better.

Now Allbirds (BIRD) is hoping that an AI overhaul can give it new life. The company said on Tuesday that it had struck an agreement for a $50 million convertible financing facility that will allow it to achieve its "long-term vision" of becoming an AI infrastructure provider.

Allbirds said it plans to change its name to NewBird AI and deploy graphics processing units as a neocloud, similar to CoreWeave (CRWV). The company said it expects to use the initial capital from its facility to acquire GPUs and provide access to customers under long-term leases, then grow over time to provide compute and other services.

"The rise of AI development and adoption has created unprecedented structural demand for specialized, high-performance compute that the market is struggling to meet," Allbirds said in a statement.

The company noted the rise in hyperscaler spending on AI and investments in AI data centers coinciding with compute shortages.

"The result is a market where enterprises, AI developers and research organizations are unable to secure the compute resources they need to build, train and run AI at scale," the company said.

The company recently agreed to sell its intellectual property and other assets to American Exchange Group.

-Britney Nguyen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Alpha Dhabi distributes cash dividends
data.description:
Staff Writer

Alpha Dhabi Holding PJSC, one of the fastest growing investment holding companies in the MENA region, listed on the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange , today announced the distribution of a cash dividend of 20 fils per share, amounting to a total of AED2 billion, for the financial year which ended 31 December 2025.

The dividend distribution was recommended by the Board of Directors and approved by shareholders at Alpha Dhabi Holdings’ Annual General Assembly held on Monday, 16 March 2026.

The approved dividend underscores the Group’s robust financial performance, strong cash generation, and disciplined capital allocation, while reaffirming Alpha Dhabi’s commitment to delivering sustainable and attractive returns to shareholders. The dividend will be distributed in a single instalment of AED2 billion on 14 April 2026.

Alpha Dhabi delivered a record financial performance in FY2025, reporting its highest full year revenue to date of AED78.8 billion, a 24 percent year on year increase. Net profit rose to AED15 billion, compared to AED13.5 billion in 2025, while adjusted EBITDA increased by 30 percent year on year to AED17.7 billion.

Building on this strong performance, and as part of the Group’s strategy to enhance shareholder returns, Alpha Dhabi announced and has since activated a share buyback programme of up to AED1 billion. This was implemented alongside a three-year dividend policy, which commenced following the close of the 2025 financial year. The policy includes the annual dividend of AED2 billion – 20 fils per share – with a planned 5 percent increase yearly. These measures underscore the company’s robust cash flow and its ongoing commitment to delivering sustainable value to its shareholders.

Eng. Hamad Al Ameri, Managing Director and Group CEO of Alpha Dhabi Holding PJSC, added, “The distribution of this dividend reflects the strength and consistency of Alpha Dhabi’s financial performance, underpinned by a diversified portfolio, strong cash flows and prudent balance sheet management. As we continue to scale our platform and execute on our long term investment strategy, our priority remains disciplined capital deployment while delivering sustainable value to our shareholders.”

Alpha Dhabi remains focused on long term value creation, leveraging its investment platform across key sectors to support earnings growth, enhance portfolio resilience, and deliver consistent returns in line with its strategic objectives.

Article Source: Emirates News Agency (WAM)

data.title: Wayve Gets $60 Million Investment From Chipmakers AMD, Arm, Qualcomm
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

U.K. self-driving technology company Wayve has raised $60 million in investments from chip companies Advanced Micro Devices, Arm Holdings and Qualcomm Ventures.

Wayve on Wednesday described the new funding as an extension of its Series D fundraising round. The company in February said it had raised $1.2 billion at an $8.6 billion valuation.

The company said it plans to use the new capital to integrate across automotive-compute platforms and support the commercial deployment of its artificial-intelligence self-driving software. It is aiming toward launching commercial robotaxi trials this year.

"We're building an AI driver that works across the full automotive compute ecosystem, from architectures already used in millions of vehicles today to the platforms powering the next generation of automated vehicles," Chief Executive Officer Alex Kendall said. "Expanding our relations with leading silicon companies helps bring that into production at a global scale."

The initial Series D funding round was led by Eclipse Capital, Balderton Capitol, and a SoftBank Group fund. The company also has backing from Microsoft, Nvidia, Uber Technologies, as well as carmakers Mercedes-Benz, Nissan Motor, and Stellantis.

Wayve has also partnered with Uber to roll out robotaxis in more than 10 cities globally, including a pilot program in Tokyo later this year in collaboration with Nissan.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Meta Stock Is the Top Pick for Tech Earnings, Morgan Stanley Says. Here's Why. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

Big technology companies will report their earnings in a couple of weeks. Meta Platforms is better positioned than Amazon.com and Google-parent Alphabet, according to Morgan Stanley.

Meta is the top pick for Morgan Stanley's Brian Nowak going into earnings, with a Buy rating and a $775 target price. On Wednesday, the stock was up 1.8% at $674.33.

Nowak says Meta trades at a discount to its large-cap peers when measured by its future earnings — around 18 times its 2027 earnings forecast. His calculation assumes a 13% compound annual earnings growth rate for Meta through 2027. That compares with around 23 times for Amazon and Alphabet.

Meta's growth will benefit from the use of AI to drive its advertising-related revenue, according to Nowak.

"We believe Meta intends to release the new MetaAI to its family of apps in the weeks ahead and will be monitoring commercial behavior as a signal of further potential long-term revenue," he wrote.

Last week, Meta launched its newest AI model, named Muse Spark. The stock got a from the relief that its expensively revamped AI team was able to launch a competitive model.

Muse Spark's capabilities rank just below the latest publicly available versions of AI models from Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic, according to research institute Epoch AI.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Fed's Hammack Prioritizing 'Persistently' High Inflation. She Says Consumers Are Struggling. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Megan Leonhardt

The labor market's anemic growth over the past year is worth keeping an eye on, but Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack says she's very focused on the fact the inflation has been too high for too long.

To combat that, Hammack said Wednesday, her baseline is that the Federal Reserve is going to have interest rates on hold "for some time." But she didn't rule out implementing rate hikes to bring monetary policy in line — nor additional cuts.

Hammack, during an appearance on CNBC's Squawk Box, doubled down on her stance that an inflation rate that's consistently above the Fed's 2% target is more of a risk than weak labor conditions. And while some economists, including Fed Chair nominee Kevin Warsh, have been optimistic that artificial intelligence could provide a disinflationary boost to the U.S. economy, Hammack is still not sold on the technology's potentially positive impact on inflation.

"We've been missing on our inflation target for five years," Hammack said. "I'm focused very much on what this means for everyday individuals. Since we've been missing on our inflation target, we've been above that 2% goal over the past five years. Individuals have experienced a decade's worth of inflation in that time period."

Hammack broke that down in real terms, saying that because of persistently above-target inflation, Americans going to the store are spending $120 on groceries when they used to spend $100 for the same cart. If inflation had been at 2%, it would be closer to $110.

Hammack is hearing from individuals in her district who are really struggling to make ends meet — they're switching from name brand products to store brands, they're buying cheaper meals and they're going without.

"I'm concerned about both sides, but from my perspective, what I see is a labor market that's reasonably in balance," Hammack said. "To me, I think that we're right around where we want to be on the labor side, but we're still persistently missing on that inflation side."

When asked about the implications of AI, Hammack said bluntly that it still isn't clear how this technology will play out in the real economy. "To me, it's still a little too early to draw a conclusion about how this is going to impact the economy overall," she said. "When I'm talking to companies in the district, almost all of them will tell me that they think they're behind the curve on AI."

She noted that she talks to a range of companies and, currently, it's only large companies that have really adopted AI in any meaningful way. Small and medium size enterprises, however, are still experimenting, but they're not using it heavily.

Beyond the everyday hardship that higher price growth brings, Hammack is also keeping consumer inflation expectations top of mind. Expectations of greater inflation can quickly become reality, fueling demand for higher wages, which in turn leads to price growth.

"Right now, inflation expectations look to be reasonably well contained, but it's important that they stay that way — and that gives us time to be patient, and to make sure that we can deliver on both sides of the mandate," Hammack said.

Hammack also called out the fact that the U.S. has suffered a series of inflationary shocks, including from higher tariffs and now the Iran war.

"All of these successive supply shocks [make it] hard to think about how we're supposed to handle those from a monetary policy perspective. Normally, you like to look through these types of supply shocks, but when it's coming on the back of already elevated inflation, it may not be the same as it would have been had we been entering this period at low and stable inflation," Hammack said.

When asked about the recent actions the Trump administration has taken against the Fed, including the prosecutors making an unannounced visit to the Fed's headquarters on Tuesday, Hammack acknowledged that there's been a "number of these threats to compromise Fed independence over the recent past." But she said it simply makes her more focused on doing her job.

"Federal Reserve Independence is really important so that I and my colleagues can continue to deliver for the American people," Hammack said. "We're independent on the policy area, but we're accountable in Congress."

Write to Megan Leonhardt at megan.leonhardt@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Nvidia Stock Heads for 11-Day Winning Streak. What It Needs to Really Break Out. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

Nvidia has been on a hot streak recently but the gains looked set to stall Wednesday. Investors waiting for a decisive break out of the chip maker's recent range might need to be patient.

Nvidia shares were down 0.5% at $195.53 in premarket trading. If the move holds, it will break a 10-day run of consecutive daily gains which has seen the stock rise from lows of around $165.

The rally has been helpful but still not the breakout to new highs that shareholders have been waiting on since a drop back from levels of more than $200 late last year and which Wall Street targets imply.

The next big hope is from Big Tech earnings, kicking off later this month. Nvidia investors will want continued increases in capital expenditure. More than that, they would like to specifically hear that new leading artificial-intelligence models are being trained on Nvidia's current-generation Blackwell hardware.

The competition point is a sore one at the moment, with multiple AI chip rivals announcing deals. Broadcom has just extended its deal with Meta Platforms, while Amazon.com has talked of selling more of its own custom AI chips to third parties.

However, Wall Street is generally confident that Nvidia will continue to be the leader in AI chips as the sector begins to focus on inference — generating output from AI models — rather than training.

"As long as the larger hyperscalers continue to buy in quantity and acknowledge that it is a superior product, Nvidia remains in a class separate from other semiconductors," said Brian Mulberry, chief market strategist at Zacks Investment Management.

"That does represent a certain amount of market share that could be at risk to new technology/processes (i.e. quantum computing) but in the near term there is no evidence of Nvidia losing that share of the market."

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Sativus Tech 2025 10-K: net loss $1.37M; EPS $(0.32)
data.description:
Sativus Tech reported a consolidated net loss of $1.365 million for the year ended December 31, 2025, compared with a net loss of $1.020 million in 2024, driving diluted and basic net loss per share attributable to equity holders to $0.32 versus $0.24 a year earlier. Operating loss increased to $1.060 million from $0.958 million in 2024, while cash and cash equivalents declined to $0.032 million at year-end. The company is transitioning its core operations toward saffron cultivation and controlled environment agriculture as part of a strategic pivot to drive future operational growth.

Financial Highlights

  • Operating loss: $1.060 million for 2025, up from $0.958 million in 2024.
  • Net loss (consolidated): $1.365 million for 2025, compared with $1.020 million in 2024.
  • Net loss attributable to equity holders: $0.869 million for 2025, versus $0.680 million in 2024.
  • Basic and diluted net loss per share attributable to equity holders: $0.32 per share for 2025, compared with $0.24 per share in 2024.
  • Cash and cash equivalents: $0.032 million at December 31, 2025, a decrease of $0.064 million year-over-year.

Business Highlights

  • Strategic pivot to saffron and controlled environment agriculture (CEA): The company shifted core operations to saffron cultivation using proprietary CEA technology aimed at enabling year-round production.
  • Proof-of-concept pilot: Preparing a commercial proof-of-concept pilot site to validate scalable and consistent saffron yields and quality in indoor controlled systems.
  • R&D and facility progress: Developed agronomic protocols and extraction methods and established an R&D center that achieves four cultivation cycles per year versus the traditional single annual cycle.
  • Partnerships and funding to support scale: Secured crowd funding, SAFEs, and a strategic investment commitment exceeding $1 million from Dreamtech to expand cultivation capacity and validate overseas proof-of-concept operations.
  • Operational outlook: Activities focus on scaling controlled environment cultivation, validating commercial yields through the pilot site, and leveraging external funding and partnerships to support expansion and overseas validation.

Original SEC Filing: SATIVUS TECH CORP. [ SATT ] - 10-K - Apr. 15, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Viant Technology Shares Rise on TVision Insights Acquisition
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Shares of Viant Technology rose after the company said it agreed to acquire TVision Insights, a company which provides television attention measurement services, in a $40 million cash-and-stock deal.

The connected TV advertising company's stock was up 11% to $11.28 in Wednesday morning trading. Shares are down 6.3% this year.

Viant plans to integrate TVision's attention tracking data into its buying platform. It said Wednesday that the addition of TVision gives its analytics platform "an unprecedented level of granularity" across several levels, increasing the return on advertising spend.

"Advertisers can now use attention, co-viewing and in-room signals within Viant's AI-powered buying platform giving them unparalleled strategic advantages, including a first-of-its-kind metric: the attention-adjusted CPM," Viant Chief Executive Officer Tim Vanderhook said.

Viant will pay $22.5 million in cash and issue $17.5 million worth of stock for the transaction, which is expected to close in the second quarter.

The company also reaffirmed its first-quarter guidance.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: BOE Likely to Keep Interest Rates on Hold in Coming Months — Market Talk
data.description:
1037 ET - The Bank of England is likely to keep interest rates on hold at 3.75% in the coming months, rather than raise interest rates, Morgan Stanley's Bruna Skarica says in a note. Investors are pricing in one quarter-point BOE interest-rate rise by September and a 40% probability of a second rate rise by year-end due to inflation concerns, LSEG data show. The Middle East conflict has raised the U.K.'s inflation risk, but a weak jobs market could limit rises in inflation, Skarica says. If the global energy supply resumes normal levels, the BOE could signal interest-rate cuts from the fourth quarter of 2026 at the earliest, she says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1015 ET - U.S. Import prices increased less than expected last month, despite sharply higher energy costs tied to the war in Iran, according to data published by the BLS. Import prices climbed 0.8% in March, while economists polled by The Wall Street Journal forecasted a 2.4% increase. Prices for import petroleum and petroleum products increased by 9.4%. Import prices for fuels and lubricants rose 2.9%, the largest monthly advance since January of last year. (jessica.coacci@wsj.com)

1011 ET - Upcoming U.K. local district elections could dampen demand for sterling if concerns about Prime Minister Keir Starmer's future resurface, Rabobank's Jane Foley says in a note. The International Monetary Fund cut its U.K. growth forecast for 2026 on Tuesday, which adds another challenge for the ruling Labour Party ahead of May local elections and parliamentary elections in Wales and Scotland, she says. A defeat for the Labour party would increase the chance of a leadership challenge for Starmer, she says. The risk of such an event could prevent speculators from betting on a stronger sterling. The euro trades flat at 0.8692 pounds. Rabobank expects it to reach 0.88 by autumn. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1009 ET - Kevin Warsh has long championed a smaller balance sheet for the Fed. But Wall Street appears skeptical that he'll be able to achieve that goal as Fed chair. Eight times a year, the New York Fed surveys major trading desks, and one of the questions solicits forecasts about how the Fed's balance sheet will change in the future. In January, before Warsh was nominated, the median response projected a Fed balance sheet of $7.384 trillion at the end of 2028. In the latest survey, the first after Warsh's nomination, the projection fell by just a hair, to $7.35 trillion. The Fed's balance sheet totaled $6.694 trillion in the latest week. So Wall Street is firm in its expectations that the Fed's assets will continue to grow, not shrink. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1009 ET - Eurozone industry likely weighed on growth in the first quarter, Ankita Amajuri at Pantheon Macroeconomics says in a note. Industrial production rose by 0.4% in February, beating expectations, but this was driven by sharp increases in Ireland and Finland, while the big four economies struggled, Amajuri says. "The carry-over for the first quarter--if output held steady in March--points to industrial production declining by 0.9% quarter to quarter, a sharp downturn relative to growth of 0.5% in the fourth quarter of last year," she says. The analyst notes that the eurozone manufacturing PMI for March proved unexpectedly strong. However, the energy-price shock and supply chain disruptions bode ill for the manufacturing sector's prospects this year, Amajuri says. (don.forbes@wsj.com)

0956 ET - The dollar gains some strength as U.S. indicators provide uplifting surprises while markets watch developments in the Middle East. Import prices rise less than expected and New York state manufacturing picks up. Goldman Sachs says in a report that bets on the U.S. dollar earlier this year have mostly been unwound. It adds that "tactical Dollar positioning has been reduced to roughly neutral levels in the past few days." The WSJ Dollar Index and ICE's DXY are both slightly up and on a path to snap a seven-day losing streak. The dollar strengthens 0.2% against the yen and the Swiss franc and 0.1% versus the euro. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0952 ET - Major cryptocurrencies are trading higher as indications of a potential end to the war in Iran has investors re-embracing assets that are typically considered riskier. CoinMarketCap's Crypto Fear and Greed index came in at 53 out of 100 yesterday, signaling a neutral sentiment. The index was at 54 on Sunday, which would tie the year-high set back in January, according to data from CoinMarketCap. Crypto markets haven't pushed into "greed" territory, or over a reading of 60, since last October. In February, the index found a year-low of 5 out of 100, indicating "extreme fear." Bitcoin is up 0.2% to roughly $74,275, while ethereum gains 0.7% to $2,332, solana is up 0.1% to $83.65, and XRP climbs 0.6% to $1.36. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0925 ET - President Trump suggests in a Fox Business interview that he will allow the criminal probe of Jerome Powell to continue even if it delays Kevin Warsh's confirmation. Trump's comments add to evidence this week that the Justice Department is not on the verge of winding down the investigation. On Tuesday, federal prosecutors visited the Washington, D.C. construction site of the Federal Reserve's headquarters renovation. That project is at the center of the probe. With only a month to go before Powell's term as Fed chair ends, the probability is growing that Warsh will not be confirmed in time to take the reins at the Fed mid-May. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

0857 ET - Treasurys sell off and yields rise as the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports challenges hopes of imminent peace. Oil prices tick higher. U.S. March import prices rise less than expected by economists surveyed by WSJ, helping ease war-related inflation fears. The New York Fed says manufacturing activity in the state increased in April, while consensus called for a decrease. Treasury Secretary Bessent says on CNBC the Fed "may have to cut more because they waited so long." The 10-year yield is at 4.270%, up from 4.255% yesterday. The two-year rises to 3.761% from 3.750%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0834 ET - The network of non-bank trading firms has expanded rapidly over the past five years, becoming more interconnected to key global banks, S&P Global Ratings' William Edwards and Giles Edwards say in a note. "A small group of banks funds trading firms and supports their liquidity needs, creating risks for major global banks," they say. A potential failure of a highly-levered trading firm could have tremendous impact on bank balance sheets, the analysts say. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0737 ET - The International Monetary Fund's downbeat forecast for the global economy is still too rosy, Oxford Economics' Ben May says in a note. The IMF's latest outlook expects 2026 global gross-domestic-product growth at 3.1%, downgraded from 3.3% in January. Oxford Economics's forecast, on a like-for-like basis, is for even slower growth of 2.9%, partly due to its expectation that oil prices will be higher than the IMF expects. Oxford Economics anticipates Brent crude prices to average $90 per barrel in 2026, about $10 per barrel higher than the IMF assumes, May says. The IMF's prediction also assumes a more upbeat view of growth prospects in advanced economies, such as the U.S., eurozone, Japan, and the U.K., he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0729 ET - The dollar turns slightly higher as oil prices climb and investors turn cautious on concerns about a U.S. blockade of Iranian ports. Eight oil tankers have reversed course on direction from U.S. forces, The Wall Street Journal reports citing a U.S. official. Ongoing disruption in the Strait of Hormuz offsets optimism over the prospect of new peace talks between the U.S. and Iran. This encourages safe-haven flows to the dollar while the currency benefits from higher oil prices due to America's energy independence. Given weekend negotiations proved unsuccessful, caution is warranted and the "balance of risks for the dollar now looks tilted to the upside," ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 98.237. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: EU Plans to Order Meta to Change WhatsApp AI Policy During Antitrust Probe — Update
data.description:
By Edith Hancock

The EU plans to order Meta Platforms to reverse a policy that officials say effectively bans rival artificial-intelligence chatbots from using its WhatsApp messaging platform as the regulator advances an antitrust investigation into the company.

"The Commission intends to impose interim measures to prevent these policy changes from causing serious and irreparable harm on the market, subject to Meta's reply and rights of defense," the European Commission said Wednesday. The injunction would be in place until it finishes its probe, the commission added.

Meta offered to let competing AI assistants use its WhatsApp business software for a fee last month in a bid to appease the commission--a move that the EU executive said still raises concerns.

Meta announced an update of its WhatsApp business terms last year that effectively banned third-party general-purpose AI assistants from using a tool to talk to users on the platform, prompting scrutiny from antitrust authorities in a number of countries.

The European Commission launched its own investigation in December.

"Pushing out competitors in fast-evolving markets like AI is exactly the type of conduct that interim measures are designed to address," Teresa Ribera, the EU's top competition enforcer, said Wednesday. "This is why we continue our proceedings towards interim measures, which would reinstate full access for rival AI assistants to WhatsApp until we have analyzed the matter in full."

A Meta spokesperson said the commission is proposing to use its regulatory powers to enable some of the world's largest companies to use its WhatsApp business product for free.

Write to Edith Hancock at edith.hancock@wsj.com
